title: The periodic table — the chemist's map
examTip: AQA most commonly tests: explaining reactivity trends in Group 1 and Group 7 in terms of electron configuration; predicting products of Group 1 reactions with water; and the difference between Mendeleev's table and the modern one.
introTips: You'll learn why the periodic table is arranged the way it is — not just alphabetically, but in a way that lets you predict how any element will behave.
Focus especially on Groups 1 and 7: AQA loves asking you to explain reactivity trends and use them to predict what an unfamiliar element will do.
The key skill is linking the number of outer electrons to chemical behaviour — once you see that connection, the whole table makes sense.
intro: The periodic table is one of the most powerful tools in all of science — not because it lists elements, but because it predicts them. When Mendeleev published his first version in 1869, he left deliberate gaps for elements he was confident existed but hadn't been discovered yet. He even predicted their properties. He was right. That's what a truly great scientific model does. Understanding the table means you can work out how any element behaves, just from its position — and that's exactly what AQA tests.

keyPoints.heading: Mendeleev and the modern table
keyPoints.content: Mendeleev (1869) arranged the elements known at the time in order of increasing atomic mass, and noticed that properties repeated in a pattern — the basis of the word 'periodic'. Crucially, he was brave enough to leave gaps where he predicted undiscovered elements should go, and he made specific predictions about those elements' properties. When they were discovered (like gallium and germanium), his predictions were remarkably accurate. The modern periodic table arranges elements in order of increasing atomic number (number of protons), not atomic mass. This small change fixed a few anomalies in Mendeleev's version and is why the table we use today works so well.
writeBullets: Mendeleev arranged by atomic mass and left gaps for undiscovered elements
He predicted properties of missing elements — later confirmed when they were found
Modern periodic table arranged by atomic number (number of protons)
Periodic = properties repeat in a regular pattern across periods

keyTerms.def: Scientist who created the first successful periodic table (1869) — arranged by atomic mass, left gaps for undiscovered elements
The number of protons in an atom's nucleus — determines an element's position in the modern periodic table
A table of elements arranged in order of increasing atomic number, in which elements with similar properties are placed in the same vertical group

keyPoints.memTip: Mendeleev = mass (old). Modern = number (atomic number). The switch from mass to number fixed the anomalies. One sentence that ties it together: 'Mendeleev was brilliant but used mass; we now use atomic number.' Say it three times, then write it without looking.

keyPoints.heading: Groups and periods — how to read the table
keyPoints.content: The periodic table is divided into vertical columns called groups and horizontal rows called periods. Groups are the most important for chemistry: all elements in the same group have the same number of outer electrons, which is why they have similar chemical properties. Group 1 elements all have 1 outer electron. Group 7 elements all have 7. Group 0 elements all have a full outer shell (8 electrons, except helium which has 2) — that's why they're unreactive. Periods tell you how many electron shells an atom has. Period 1 elements have 1 shell; Period 2 have 2 shells; Period 3 have 3 shells — and so on. Moving across a period, you're filling up the same shell one electron at a time.
writeBullets: Groups = vertical columns; elements in same group have same number of outer electrons
Group number = number of outer electrons (for Groups 1–7)
Periods = horizontal rows; period number = number of electron shells
Group 0 = full outer shell → stable, unreactive noble gases

keyTerms.def: A vertical column in the periodic table — elements in the same group have the same number of outer electrons and similar chemical properties
A horizontal row in the periodic table — the period number tells you how many electron shells the atoms have

keyPoints.memTip: Groups go DOWN, periods go ACROSS. 'Group number = outer electrons' is the rule that explains almost everything. Test yourself: how many outer electrons does a Group 5 element have? (Five.) How many shells does a Period 3 element have? (Three.) Drill this until it's automatic.

keyPoints.heading: Metals and non-metals
keyPoints.content: Metals sit on the left and centre of the periodic table. Non-metals sit on the right. A zigzag line divides them, running roughly between Groups 3 and 4 from boron down to astatine. Elements on or near this boundary are called metalloids (or semi-metals) — they have properties of both metals and non-metals. Silicon is the most famous: it's a semiconductor, which is why it's used in computer chips. Metals typically: conduct electricity, are shiny, malleable, and form positive ions. Non-metals typically: are poor conductors, dull in appearance, and form negative ions or share electrons in covalent bonds.
writeBullets: Metals: left and centre of the table — conduct electricity, malleable, form positive ions
Non-metals: right of the table — poor conductors, form negative ions or covalent bonds
Zigzag line separates metals from non-metals
Metalloids (e.g. silicon) sit on the boundary — have properties of both

keyTerms.def: An element with properties intermediate between metals and non-metals — e.g. silicon; used as semiconductors
A material that conducts electricity under some conditions but not others — e.g. silicon in computer chips

keyPoints.memTip: Picture the table split by a staircase (the zigzag line): climb the stairs from bottom-left to top-right and you're crossing from metals to non-metals. Silicon sits right on the staircase — it's the famous semiconductor. Sketch the staircase once to fix it in your memory.

keyPoints.heading: Group 1 — the alkali metals
keyPoints.content: Group 1 (lithium, sodium, potassium...) are called the alkali metals. They each have 1 outer electron, which they readily give away to form a +1 ion — that's what makes them so reactive. When they react with water, they produce a metal hydroxide and hydrogen gas: for example, sodium + water → sodium hydroxide + hydrogen. Here's the key trend: reactivity increases as you go down Group 1. As you move down, each element has more electron shells, so the outer electron is further from the nucleus and less strongly attracted. It's easier to lose, so the element is more reactive. Potassium is more reactive than sodium, which is more reactive than lithium.
writeBullets: Group 1 = alkali metals — 1 outer electron, readily lost to form +1 ion
React with water → metal hydroxide + hydrogen gas
Reactivity INCREASES down Group 1
Reason: outer electron further from nucleus down the group → less attracted → easier to lose

keyTerms.def: Group 1 elements — highly reactive metals that each have 1 outer electron and react with water to form metal hydroxides and hydrogen
The salt-like compound (e.g. sodium hydroxide, NaOH) formed when an alkali metal reacts with water

keyPoints.memTip: Group 1 reactivity: DOWN = MORE reactive. The outer electron gets further from the nucleus, so there's less attraction holding it — it escapes more easily. Imagine the nucleus as a magnet: the further the electron, the weaker the pull. Lithium < Sodium < Potassium < Rubidium < Caesium — write the trend with an upward arrow next to it.

keyPoints.heading: Group 7 and Group 0
keyPoints.content: Group 7 (the halogens) each have 7 outer electrons and need just one more to achieve a full outer shell — which is why they react by gaining an electron. They exist as diatomic molecules: F₂, Cl₂, Br₂, I₂ (two atoms bonded together). Unlike Group 1, reactivity in Group 7 decreases as you go down the group. Going down, the outer shell is further from the nucleus and there are more inner electron shells shielding it — so incoming electrons are attracted less strongly, making it harder to gain that extra electron. Group 0 (the noble gases) have full outer shells — 8 electrons (2 for helium) — so they have no need to gain or lose electrons. This is why they are virtually unreactive.
writeBullets: Group 7 = halogens — 7 outer electrons, react by gaining 1 electron
Halogens are diatomic: F₂, Cl₂, Br₂, I₂
Reactivity DECREASES down Group 7 (outer shell further away, harder to attract an electron)
Group 0 = noble gases — full outer shell → unreactive

keyTerms.def: Group 7 elements — reactive non-metals that exist as diatomic molecules and react by gaining one electron
A molecule made of exactly two atoms bonded together — e.g. Cl₂, F₂
Group 0 elements — unreactive because they have a full outer electron shell

keyPoints.memTip: Group 1: down = MORE reactive (losing gets easier). Group 7: down = LESS reactive (gaining gets harder). The trends are OPPOSITE — and that's the AQA trick. Write them side by side with arrows pointing in opposite directions. Stick it somewhere you'll see it. Fluorine is the most reactive halogen; iodine is the least.

tables.title: Reactivity trends in Group 1 and Group 7
rows: Outer electron is further from nucleus → less attraction → easier to lose
Outer shell is further from nucleus → harder to attract an extra electron

2.question: Elements in the same group of the periodic table have similar chemical properties. What is the best explanation for this?
options: They have the same mass number
They have the same number of electron shells
They have the same number of outer electrons
2.correctFeedback: Correct — the number of outer electrons determines chemical behaviour. That's why every element in Group 1 has 1 outer electron, and all of them react similarly.
2.wrongFeedback: Chemical properties are determined by outer electrons, not total shells or mass. Elements in the same group have the same number of outer electrons — that's what makes them behave similarly.

4.question: Potassium reacts more vigorously with water than sodium does. What is the best explanation for this?
options: Potassium has more protons so it is heavier
Potassium's outer electron is in a shell further from the nucleus and is more easily lost
Potassium has more neutrons so releases more energy
Potassium is a liquid at room temperature
4.correctFeedback: Exactly right. Potassium is below sodium in Group 1, so its outer electron is in a higher shell and further from the nuclear pull — it's lost more easily, making potassium more reactive.
4.wrongFeedback: The key is the outer electron. Going down Group 1, each element has an extra electron shell, so the outer electron is further from the nucleus and less attracted to it. It's easier to lose, so the element is more reactive.

commonMistakes: The modern periodic table is arranged by atomic NUMBER (protons), not atomic mass. Mendeleev used atomic mass — the modern table does not.
Group 1 reactivity increases DOWN the group. Group 7 reactivity decreases DOWN the group. These trends are opposite — get them clear before the exam.

The reason Group 1 gets more reactive going down is that the outer electron is further from the nucleus and shielded by more inner shells, making it easier to lose — not because the atom is bigger or heavier.
Halogens are diatomic in their elemental form: fluorine is F₂, chlorine is Cl₂, bromine is Br₂, iodine is I₂. Writing F or Cl on its own is incorrect for the elemental form.
Noble gases (Group 0) are unreactive because they have a full outer shell of electrons — not because they 'don't want to react' or 'have no electrons to share'.
examTips: For any reactivity trend question in Groups 1 or 7: you must explain using electron configuration — state that the outer shell is further from the nucleus and explain whether that makes gaining or losing an electron easier or harder.
When writing the Group 1 + water reaction, remember both products: metal hydroxide AND hydrogen gas. Missing one product drops a mark.
For Mendeleev questions: three things to mention — arranged by atomic mass, left gaps for unknown elements, predicted their properties. All three are usually needed for full marks.
revisionCardBullets: Mendeleev (1869): arranged by atomic mass, left gaps, predicted undiscovered elements
Modern periodic table: arranged by atomic NUMBER (protons)
Groups = vertical columns; same group = same number of outer electrons = similar chemical properties
Group number = number of outer electrons (Groups 1–7)
Periods = horizontal rows; period number = number of electron shells
Metals (left) / non-metals (right) / metalloids on zigzag boundary (e.g. silicon)
Group 1 (alkali metals): 1 outer electron; react with water → metal hydroxide + hydrogen
Group 1 reactivity INCREASES down the group (outer electron further from nucleus, easier to lose)
Group 7 (halogens): 7 outer electrons; diatomic molecules (F₂, Cl₂, Br₂, I₂)
Group 7 reactivity DECREASES down the group (outer shell further away, harder to gain an electron)
Group 0 (noble gases): full outer shell → unreactive
